title: How Can I Deal With Serious Imposter Syndrome at School?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You look around the classroom. Everyone seems confident. They answer questions without hesitation. They finish exams early. You feel like you're faking it. You're waiting to be exposed as a fraud. This is imposter syndrome. It's not truth. It's a feeling. And it's incredibly common. Here is how to shake it off.

You Are Not Alone

Up to 70% of people experience imposter syndrome at some point. Successful people experience it more often than unsuccessful people. The more you achieve, the more you might feel like a fraud.

Michelle Obama has talked about imposter syndrome. Tom Hanks has. Maya Angelou said, 'I have written eleven books, but each time I think, 'Uh oh, they're going to find out now. I've run a game on everybody.'

If the most accomplished people feel this way, it's not a sign of incompetence. It's a sign of caring about your work.

Feelings Are Not Facts

You feel like you don't belong. That does not mean you don't belong. Feelings are not evidence. They are reactions. You can acknowledge the feeling without believing it.

When imposter thoughts arise, label them. 'I'm having the thought that I don't belong here.' Notice the thought. Don't fight it. Don't believe it. Just notice it. Then let it pass.

Look at the Evidence

You were admitted to this school. You passed the entrance requirements. You have earned passing grades. These are facts. Your imposter feelings are not.

Write down your accomplishments. Read them when you feel like a fraud. The evidence will contradict the feeling. Use it.

Stop Comparing Your Insides to Others' Outsides

You see your own struggles, doubts, and failures. You see others' confidence, success, and ease. You don't see their struggles, doubts, and failures. Everyone hides them.

The student who answers every question might be terrified of being called on. The student who finishes exams early might be guessing. You don't know. Assume they're as anxious as you are. You'll probably be right.

Talk About It

Say to a friend: 'I've been feeling like I don't belong here. Do you ever feel that way?' They will almost certainly say yes. The secret is out. You're not alone. The shame of secrecy is worse than the feeling itself.

If you can't talk to a friend, talk to a counselor. Most colleges offer free mental health services. Use them. This is what they're for.

Own Your Success

When someone compliments you, say 'thank you.' Don't deflect. Don't explain why you didn't deserve it. You did deserve it. Accept the compliment. Practice saying 'thank you' until it feels normal.

Keep a file of positive feedback. Emails from professors. Comments on papers. Performance reviews. Read them when you feel like a fraud.

Reframe Failure as Learning

Imposter syndrome makes you afraid of failure. Failure, they say, will expose you. The truth is that failure is normal. Everyone fails. The question is what you learn from it.

When you fail at something, ask: 'What can I learn from this?' Not 'What does this say about me?' The first question leads to growth. The second leads to shame.

You belong here. You earned your place. The feeling of being a fraud is not evidence. It's a symptom of caring. Acknowledge it. Talk about it. Keep going.
